data.title: Executive reshuffles: INTC, ZS, ETSY and SEDG
data.description:
Market watchers, throughout the week, observed several Wall Street executives stepping up for new roles as part of leadership reshuffling.

Meanwhile, some companies such as Intel, Zscaler and Etsy added new positions to steer growth prospects.

Here are some of the recent management changes that took place during the week:

Intel (NASDAQ:INTC) made the surprising announcement on Monday that CEO Pat Gelsinger is retiring and will be replaced by co-CEOs David Zinsner and Michelle Johnston Holthaus. Gelsinger is also stepping down from the company's board of directors. Both departures are effective Dec. 1.

Zscaler (NASDAQ:ZS) CFO Remo Canessa is retiring after serving in the position for nearly eight years.

Etsy (NASDAQ:ETSY) said Lanny Baker will join the company as CFO. Baker, who will succeed Rachel Glaser, is currently COO and previously CFO of Eventbrite.

SolarEdge Technologies (SEDG) said that it appointed Chief Marketing Officer Shuki Nir as its new CEO effective immediately, succeeding Ronen Faier, who has served as interim CEO since August.

Humana (HUM) has announced that Celeste Mellet will be appointed to succeed Susan Diamond as CFO, effective Jan 11, 2025.

Retail discount stores company Dollar Tree (DLTR) said that its CFO Jeffrey Davis will step down from the role, effective upon the appointment of a successor.

Joby Aviation (JOBY) said that Matthew Field, the company’s CFO, would be resigning, effective Dec. 13, 2024, for personal reasons.

Cerence (CRNC) announced that it has appointed Antonio Rodriquez as its permanent CFO, effective Nov. 29, 2024.

Fluor (FLR) has announced that executive vice president and CFO, Joe Brennan, will retire effective July 1, 2025. The Board has appointed John Regan as the new finance chief, effective March 1, 2025.

Beam Therapeutics (BEAM) has appointed Sravan K. Emany as CFO, effective Dec. 19, 2024. He most recently served as CFO and COO at Ironwood Pharmaceuticals (IRWD).

data.title: Trump says U.S. should not get involved in conflict in Syria
data.description:
President-elect Donald Trump said on Saturday that the U.S. should not be involved in a fight in Syria, where rebel forces opposed to the government of President Bashar Al-Assad have retaken a string of cities.

"Syria is a mess, but is not our friend, & THE UNITED STATES SHOULD HAVE NOTHING TO DO WITH IT. THIS IS NOT OUR FIGHT. LET IT PLAY OUT. DO NOT GET INVOLVED!," Trump wrote in a post on Truth Social.

Trump said that Russia, an Assad ally, "seems incapable of stopping this literal march through Syria, a country they have protected for years," because of Moscow's involvement in Ukraine.

Russia being forced out of Ukraine "may actually be the best thing that can happen to them," Trump said. "There was never much of a benefit in Syria for Russia."

About 900 U.S. troops are currently based in Syria, primarily in the northeastern part of the country, as part of a mission to prevent the resurgence of the Islamic State.

Russian forces intervened militarily in Syria in 2015, tilting the conflict in favor of the Assad government. Earlier in the week, the Kremlin said that it is continuing to support Assad.

Trump announced in 2018 that he wanted to withdraw U.S. troops from Syria, but held off later on.

The U.S. State Department did not immediately respond to a Seeking Alpha request for comment.

data.title: Crypto names Coinbase, IREN rule weekly financial movers in banner week for bitcoin
data.description:
With bitcoin (BTC) breaking the $100K mark late Wednesday, crypto names stood out among the week's best-performing financial stocks. President-elect Donald Trump's nomination of Paul Atkins, known for his pro-crypto stance, pushed the token above what had been a psychological resistance level.

Crypto fared much better than equity markets, with the S&P 500 rising only 0.96% for the week. Financial stocks faltered, with the Financial Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLF) slipped 1.8% during the week.

By contrast, bitcoin (BTC-USD) climbed 3.9% since last Friday.

Coinbase Global (NASDAQ:COIN) stock jumped 16% for the week, as investors believe it will face fewer regulatory burdens in operating its crypto exchange under a second Trump administration.

IREN Ltd (NASDAQ:IREN), which announced an upsized offering of senior notes, gained 14%.

Robinhood Markets (NASDAQ:HOOD), which lists crypto tokens on its trading app, rose 11%. The stock was also upgraded by Goldman this week, and the company's CEO said it's looking into sports betting.

Canada's Bank of Montreal (NYSE:BMO), which raised its dividend and announced a 20M-share buyback, increased 8.6%, even though fiscal Q4 earnings missed the consensus estimate.

Hut 8 (NASDAQ:HUT), a bitcoin miner and high-performance computing infrastructure company, moved up 7.9% for the week.

The week's biggest decliners were led by payment tech company Sezzle (NASDAQ:SEZL), which sank 15% for the week.

With political turmoil in South Korea, KB Financial (NYSE:KB) slid 13%.

Insurtech name Lemonade (NYSE:LMND) dropped 9.2%.

StepStone Group (NASDAQ:STEP), a private equity and venture capital firm, dipped 8.6%.

TD Bank Group (NYSE:TD) stock wilted 8.1% in the week that it suspended its medium-term financial targets as it starts a strategic review and focuses on remediating weaknesses in its anti-money laundering controls.

data.title: 9 out of 12 companies deliver EPS wins this week-Earnings Scorecard
data.description:
Wall Street's major averages were up on Friday after a week marked by robust earnings and strong economic data fuelled investors with optimism.

Non-farm payrolls resurged in November, making up for October's storms, indicating that the labor market remains solid. The U.S. economy added 227K jobs last month, topping the +211K consensus estimate.

On the earnings front, out of the 12 S&P 500 companies that reported earnings this week, nine of them delivered strong EPS wins. Two companies beat revenue estimates, while only one missed estimates for both EPS and revenue.

Consumer companies were at the forefront this week after a successful Thanksgiving and Black Friday, as American consumers continued to turn out in droves to shop. Spending reached a record $10.8B on Black Friday, according to Adobe's data, marking a Y/Y rise of 10.2%. That came after a total spend of $6.1B on Thanksgiving.

Seven out of eight consumer companies that reported earnings this week, beat estimates, alleviating worries over the state of the U.S. economy, which was widely cited as one of the top concerns among voters during the election.

Companies such as athleisure retailer, Lululemon delivered solid quarterly results, with better-than-expected profit and sales, and double-digit comparable sales growth in its international markets.

The company also raised its FY24 guidance above Wall Street’s expectations and raised its share buyback program by another billion dollars.

Along similar lines, another consumer giant, Dollar Tree (NASDAQ:DLTR) also beat top and bottom-line estimates for the third quarter, and updated its forecast. For the fiscal year 2024, adjusted earnings per share are now expected in the $5.31 to $5.51 range, revised from $5.20 to $5.60.Net sales are expected to be $30.7B to $30.9B.

In contrast to these upbeat forecasts, retail giant Kroger (NYSE:KR) missed its revenue estimates due to the impact of lower fuel prices and the sale of its specialty pharmacy business. Kroger (NYSE:KR) also narrowed its EPS guidance to a range of $4.35 to $4.45 per share from $4.30 to $4.50 per share.

Three companies from the technology sector also reported earnings this week, with two of them beating estimates. Hewlett Packard (NYSE:HPE) and Synopsys (NASDAQ:SNPS) beat estimates for both revenue and EPS, while Salesforce (NYSE:CRM) missed EPS expectations.

HP Enterprise (HPE) reported fiscal fourth-quarter results and guidance that topped Wall Street's expectations, aided in part by the artificial intelligence spending boom. For the period ending October 31, HP Enterprise said it earned an adjusted $0.58 per share as revenue rose 15.6% year-over-year to come in at $8.5B.

On the other hand, Salesforce (CRM) reported adjusted earnings per share of $2.41, which was less than the consensus estimate of $2.45. The company said the lower-than-expected earnings per share was due to losses on strategic investments.

For the upcoming week, six S&P500 companies are scheduled to report earnings, with Oracle (NYSE:ORCL) reporting on Monday, AutoZone (NYSE:AZO) on Tuesday, and Adobe (NASDAQ:ADBE) on Wednesday. Thursday will include names like Broadcom (NASDAQ:AVGO), and Costco (NASDAQ:COST).

data.title: Key deals this week: Shell-Equinor U.K. combination, Hershey, TotalEnergies, GE HealthCare and more
data.description:
Here's a list of key deals reported this week across sectors:

Conagra (NYSE:CAG) is looking to sell its Chef Boyardee pasta business.

Arcadia Biosciences (NASDAQ:RKDA) is set to merge with the Dallas-based oil and gas company Roosevelt Resources in an all-stock deal.

British oil giant Shell (NYSE:SHEL) and Norway's state-run energy firm Equinor (NYSE:EQNR) will combine their U.K. offshore oil and gas assets to form a new company to be the U.K.'s largest independent oil and gas producer.

Shares of chocolate maker Hershey (NYSE:HSY) seesawed on Wednesday, closing down almost 1%, amid some takeover speculation.

TotalEnergies (NYSE:TTE) said Wednesday it agreed to acquire German renewable project developer VSB Group from P-E firm Partners Group Holding for €1.57B (~$1.6B), adding more than 2 GW of developed onshore wind power capacity across Europe.

GXO Logistics (NYSE:GXO) dropped 11% in after-hours trading amid a report that the company will remain independent after rejecting takeover offers. The company also announced that Chief Executive Officer Malcolm Wilson will retire next year.

Private equity firm TPG (NASDAQ:TPG) is examining a possible sale of Crunch Fitness that may value the gym chain at $1.5 billion, including debt.

GE HealthCare (NASDAQ:GEHC) has agreed to acquire the remaining 50% stake in Nihon Medi-Physics (NMP) from Sumitomo Chemical (OTCPK:SOMMY), aiming to assume full ownership of the Japanese radiopharmaceutical company.

Northern Star Resources (OTCPK:NESRF) said early Monday it agreed to acquire Australian gold mining peer De Grey Mining (OTCPK:DGMLF) in an all-stock deal valued at ~A$5B (~$3.26B).

data.title: Trending stocks amid week of new highs for Wall Street
data.description:
Multiple key indices had a strong week in the first week of December, with the benchmark S&P 500 hitting a record high and rising 0.8% over the five days of trading.

The index was buoyed by sentiment of a December rate cut following a U.S. jobs report for November that showed a slowing yet solid labor market.

Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) also reached a new closing high, boosted by November nonfarm payroll data. The tech-focused Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) jumped 2.6%, while the Dow (DJI) dropped 0.3%.

This week also saw bitcoin (BTC-USD) cross $100,000 for the first time after several days near the milestone. The major cryptocurrency has jumped over 125% so far this year.

As the market prepares to wind down the year on a strong note, these were among the trending stocks:

Marvell Technology (MRVL) shares jumped on Wednesday to hit an all-time high after the chipmaker reported fiscal third-quarter results and guidance that topped expectations, due in part to artificial intelligence.

SentinelOne (S) shares tumbled 15% on Thursday in its biggest drop since May after the cybersecurity software firm reported third quarter adj. EPS that missed the average analyst estimate.

Salesforce (CRM US) shares advanced 9% after the software company reported fiscal third-quarter results. Despite somewhat mixed results and guidance, Salesforce demonstrated consistent growth in revenue and subscriptions, up 8% and 9% year over year, respectively.

U.S. listed shares of Korea-based companies fell sharply after South Korea's President Yoon imposed a martial law in a sudden late-night address to the country that was later lifted. KB Financial Group (KB) fell 12%, Coupang (CPNG) -6% and Posco Holdings (PKX) dropped 7.4%.

Airline stocks registered gains this week after American Airlines (AAL) +20% and Southwest Airlines (LUV) +5% provided upbeat forecasts, a positive for the sector indicating an uptick in air travel for the ongoing holiday season. JetBlue (JBLU) shares also jumped 9% as the airline boosted its revenue forecast for the fourth quarter.

Intel (INTC) dropped 16% as investors continued to wonder about the fate of the semiconductor giant following CEO Pat Gelsinger's departure. Earlier in the week, Intel (INTC) made the surprising announcement that Pat Gelsinger is retiring and will be replaced by co-CEOs David Zinsner and Michelle Johnston Holthaus.

Super Micro Computer (SMCI) added 16% this week after an independent special committee found no evidence of misconduct by the board of directors or audit committee. The company also received an extension from Nasdaq to file its pending annual and quarterly reports.

UnitedHealth Group (UNH) shares tumbled 9% in the aftermath of the shooting death of the head of its UnitedHealthcare division. UnitedHealthcare CEO Brian Thompson was shot and killed earlier Wednesday morning outside a midtown Manhattan hotel where he was scheduled to speak at the company's annual investor conference. The news has shaken the healthcare industry, with shares of many health insurers down on Friday.

AT&T (T) was in focus after the telecom giant gave a stronger-than-expected outlook for the coming years and set a $20B stock buyback plan. The wireless carrier expects a double-digit percentage growth in adjusted EPS and free cash flow of more than $18B in 2027 as it embarked on a multi-year strategic plan.

Microchip Technology (MCHP) fell 13% after the chipmaker said it planned to shut down a plant in Arizona and said it expects third-quarter revenue being close to the low end of its original guidance. The company is also reportedly pausing its application for U.S. chip subsidies. It was in line for $162M in grants to support facilities in Oregon and Colorado.

Palantir Technologies (PLTR) rose 13% in the week and was among the most traded stocks in the U.S. market this week, alongside SoundHound (SOUN) +63%. SoundHound (SOUN) gained after the company presented its artificial intelligence-powered, conversational voice products at a conference.

Toast (TOST) fell 10% after the company's chief financial officer unexpectedly lowered expectations for 2025 adjusted EBITDA margins at an investment conference. CFO Elena Gomez indicated 100-200 basis points of expansion for adjusted EBITDA as a percentage of fintech and subscription gross profit, compared with consensus embedding 360 bps of expansion.

Zscaler (ZS) was in focus after the security software company gave a forecast for adjusted second-quarter earnings that fell short of Wall Street estimates.

data.title: Credit Agricole had informal backing of Italian government for BPM stake increase: report
data.description:
French banking group Credit Agricole (OTCPK:CRARF) had the informal backing of Italy’s government before announcing it would raise its stake in Banco BPM (OTCPK:BNCZF), Reuters reported Saturday, citing two sources close to the matter.

Credit Agricole said Friday it has entered into derivatives contracts to raise its stake in BPM to 15.1% from its current 9.9%. It added that it is seeking approval from the European Central Bank to acquire up to 19.99% of BPM.

Credit Agricole said it will not make a full takeover offer of BPM.

Meanwhile, Reuters reported Saturday that a source familiar with Credit Agricole’s strategy said the bank is aiming to bolster its negotiating position to defend commercial pacts that generate income in its largest market outside of France.

UniCredit (OTCPK:UNCFF)(OTCPK:UNCRY) last month made a bid to acquire BPM, undermining a plan by the Italian government to have BPM and state-backed Monte dei Paschi di Siena (OTCPK:BMDPF) combine.

Neither Credit Agricole nor BPM immediately responded to requests for comment by Seeking Alpha.

data.title: Real estate stocks slump despite rising December rate cut hopes
data.description:
Real estate stocks slid down despite rising odds of a rate cut in December 2024.

Nonfarm payrolls resurged in November, making up for October's storms and the Boeing strike and then some, indicating that the labor market remains solid, data released by the U.S. Department of Labor said on Friday.

Major market averages found upside support this week from rising expectations that the Federal Reserve will continue chopping down interest rates at its December 17-18 meeting.

However, The Real Estate Select Sector SPDR Fund ETF (NYSEARCA:XLRE) fell 2.73% to close the week at 43.77. The fund logged losses in all five trading sessions.

The Dow Jones REIT Indx Equity REIT Total Return Index (NASDAQ:REIT:IND) was down 2.40% from last week, while FTSE Nareit All Equity REITs (INDEXFTSE: FNER) retreated by 2.81%.

"REITs are seen as income investments with the potential for capital gains. They are closely related to long bonds such as the iShares 20+ Year Treasury Bond ETF (TLT) which has the same qualities," noted Seeking Alpha author MacroGirl.

Seeking Alpha analysts grade XLRE as Buy, while the Quant Rating system rates it as Hold.

Company News

  • BMO Capital Markets lowered its recommendation on the industrial REIT Prologis (PLD), citing its valuation "screens expensive". Office REITs JBG SMITH Properties (JBGS) and Cousins Properties (CUZ) were also downgraded.
  • Ventas (VTR) said tenant Brookdale Senior Living didn't exercise its right to extend the term of its master lease for renewal starting Jan. 1, 2026.
  • Specialized REIT Iron Mountain (IRM) plans to offer $750M of senior notes due 2033 in a private placement.
  • Extra Space Storage (EXR) said its operating partnership, Extra Space Storage LP, has priced a public offering of $300M aggregate principal amount of additional 5.700% senior notes due 2028.
  • Traffic at Simon Property Group's (SPG) shopping centers in the U.S. climbed 6.4% Y/Y, amid preliminary reports of surging sales from retailers.
  • Camden Property Trust (CPT), Prologis (PLD), VICI Properties (VICI), American Tower (AMT), and Essex Property Trust (ESS) declared dividends this week.

Performance

Life sciences-focused REIT Alexandria Real Estate Equities (ARE) was the biggest loser of the week. ARE has largely been on a downward trajectory after J.P. Morgan downgraded the stock and lowered its FFO estimates as leasing demand remains mediocre in the segment.

CoStar Group (CSGP) lost 3.41% on Thursday, December 5, the day the company held its investor conference. The real estate services provider was the second-biggest loser of the week.

Hotel & Resort REITs benefited by media reports about Trinity Investments expressing takeover interest in Sunstone Hotel Investors (SHO) this week. Host Hotels & Resorts (HST) ended the week in green.

Here is a look at the subsector performances for the week:

data.title: Insider trades: Goldman Sachs, Abercrombie & Fitch among notable names this week
data.description:
A weekly summary of insider trading highlighting notable buying and selling by investors, directors and executives. The trades have taken place between December 2 and December 6, 2024.

• Banking giant, Goldman Sachs Group (NYSE:GS) said in a filing that its CEO and Chairman of Board, David M. Solomon, sold 6,550 common shares at $610.91 per share for a total transaction value of about $4M. Solomon now owns about 117,883 shares.

• Realty Income Corp (NYSE:O) reported a recent sale transaction this week. As disclosed on December 4, 2024, Director Gregory McLaughlin sold 1,250 shares of common stock at a price of $56.17 per share, totaling $70,212. McLaughlin now holds about 34,636 shares of the company.

• Retailer, Abercrombie & Fitch (NYSE:ANF), also disclosed that insiders sold about $3M shares this week. Suzanne M. Coulter, director, sold 6,800 shares of the company's Class A common stock at a price of $161.802 per share, for a total transaction value of approximately $1.1M. Executive VP, Gregory Henchel, sold about $1.5M worth of the company’s stock at $150 per share. Kenneth Robinson, director of the company, also sold about 1,100 class A common stock for a price of $161.74 per share. Robinson now holds 7,272 class A stock of the company.

• Emiliano Calemzuk, Director at MercadoLibre (NASDAQ:MELI), disclosed through a filing that he sold 50 shares of the company at a price of $1984.98 per share for total worth $99,249. Calemzuk now holds 238 shares of the company.

•In a couple of transactions, M Grier Eliasek, Chief Operating Officer of Prospect Capital (NASDAQ:PSEC), expanded holdings in the company by purchasing its shares. Eliasek acquired 210,000 shares at $4.79 per share for value of $1M. Eliasek also acquired another 210,000 shares at $4.81 per share for value of $1M. With this, the COO’s total holdings in the company comes up to 2.1M.

• Michelle Sterling, Director at Digital Turbine (NASDAQ:APPS), purchased 7,000 shares of the company at $1.43 per share, totaling a value of $10,010. Sterling now holds about 24,640 shares of APPS.

data.title: Top Boeing lobbyist Ziad Ojakli leaving planemaker: reports
